title: Characterizing electronic scattering rates with transport in multiterminal devices
abstract: Strongly interacting electrons in clean two-dimensional devices are theorized to exhibit many distinct transport regimes, such as ballistic, hydrodynamic, or diffusive. Realistic samples often lie in crossover regimes between these idealized limits. We show how a single experiment on a multiterminal device can distinguish these regimes and constrain the relevant scattering rates without space-resolved imaging. Using a linearized Boltzmann model in a five-terminal geometry, we find that current partition among the drain contacts diagnoses the ballistic-hydrodynamic-Ohmic crossover and allows extraction of momentum-relaxing and momentum-conserving scattering rates in the crossover regime. The same geometry also exhibits clear signatures of the tomographic regime, potentially allowing for a quantitative discrimination between viscous and tomographic flow in experiments. Our results demonstrate that multiterminal devices are a simpler experimental route to characterize transport regimes in electron liquids, relative to space-resolved imaging experiments.
summary: This paper proposes a method to characterize electronic transport regimes and scattering rates in 2D materials using simple multi-terminal resistance measurements. By analyzing how current splits between different contacts, researchers can distinguish between ballistic, hydrodynamic, and diffusive flow without needing complex imaging techniques. This provides a robust way to study electron liquids and identify the onset of the tomographic regime.
main_problem: The difficulty of quantitatively distinguishing between various electronic transport regimes (ballistic, hydrodynamic, tomographic, and diffusive) and extracting microscopic scattering rates without using space-resolved imaging.
main_result: A five-terminal 'fan' geometry allows for the extraction of momentum-relaxing and momentum-conserving scattering rates through current partition measurements, providing a simpler alternative to imaging.
method: Numerical solution of the linearized Boltzmann transport equation using the FermiSea.jl framework and a spectral method with harmonic truncation.
model_or_system: Electrons in clean, two-dimensional (2D) conductors modeled as an isotropic Fermi liquid near T = 0.
key_observables: Current partition (branching ratios) among drain contacts, specifically the fractions I_a/I_N, and nonlocal resistances.
important_parameters: Momentum-relaxing scattering rate (gamma_mr), momentum-conserving electron-electron scattering rate (gamma_ee), and the parameter gamma_3 for higher-order mode relaxation.
main_assumptions: Focuses on the T -> 0 limit, assumes an isotropic Fermi liquid, and primarily assumes purely diffuse boundary conditions.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the device geometry and current density; Fig 2 displays heatmaps of current fractions as functions of scattering rates and Jacobian diagnostics; Fig 4 shows nonlocal resistance and sensitivity/condition number analysis.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of transport regime identification, describes the five-terminal device geometry and the Boltzmann modeling approach, presents results for regime differentiation and parameter extraction, and concludes with a discussion on the sensitivity of the inverse problem.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the five-terminal ‘fan’ geometry stud- ied in this work. The colour map represents the calculated current magnitude |j(x)| obtained from the linearized Boltz- mann equation (2) with γmr = 0, γee = γ3 = 100 vF /L in (4). The drain arms are chosen to have different widths and lengths, while w/ℓis held fixed for each arm so that they represent identical ohmic channels. The relative arm dimen- sions are ℓN/L = ℓS/L = 0.35, ℓNE/L = 0.37, ℓE/L = 0.32, ℓSE/L = 0.28, with wa/ℓa = 0.30 for for all arms. The relative angles are 45◦.

FIG. 2. Current partition in the five-terminal fan geometry as a function of momentum-relaxing and momentum-conserving scattering. (a)–(c): current Ia for a = NE, E, SE, as a fraction of IN, the current through the north contact, IN. (d): smallest singular value σmin(J) of the Jacobian J = ∂(INE/IN, IE/IN, ISE/IN)/∂(log10 γmr, log10 γee), measuring the weakest local sensitivity of the observables to the scattering rates. (e): condition number κ(J), measuring the local degeneracy of the inverse map. The crossover regime is the most favourable for extracting both γmr and γee from multiterminal current measurements, while the strongly Ohmic regime becomes ill-conditioned.

FIG. 3. Emergence of tomographic physics. We set γmr = 0 and consider the fraction Ia/|IN| for a = SE, E, NE. (a): Sample trajectories in phase space assuming γee ∼T 2 and γ3 ∼T 4, so that γ3 = γ2 ee/ξ, with ξ controlling the strength of even-odd effects. At large ξ (tomographic regime), the trajectory in (INE/IN, ISE/IN)-space develops a sharp feature as T is swept. (b)–(d): Ia/IN as a function of γ3/γee, artificially treating them as independent parameters, expressing a rich tomographic enhancement or suppression of current branching ratios.

FIG. 4. Nonlocal resistance in the fan geometry as a function of momentum-relaxing and momentum-conserving scattering. In contrast to Fig. 1, here a fixed current IS = IE = I is driven around a sharp corner, and the nonlocal resistance at N, NE, SE reported. (a)–(c): Resistance Ra ≡Va/IS, in units of µ0e−2n−1 0 v−1 F w−1 S , for a = N, NE, SE. (d): smallest singular value σmin(J) of the Jacobian J = ∂(RN, RNE, RSE)/∂(log10 γmr, log10 γee), measuring the weakest local sensitivity of the observables to the scattering rates.
